blocks:
Ayahs

يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْمَلِكِ ٱلْقُدُّوسِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and in the earth glorifies Allah, the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى بَعَثَ فِى ٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢)
It is He who sent to the unlettered [people] an apostle from among themselves, to recite to them His signs, to purify them, and to teach them the Book and wisdom, and earlier they had indeed been in manifest error. (2)
وَءَاخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِمْ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣)
And to others from among them [as well] who have not yet joined them. And He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (3)
ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٤)
That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (4)
مَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ ٱلْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًۢا ۚ بِئْسَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥)
The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then failed to carry it, is that of an ass carrying books. Evil is the example of the people who deny Allah’s signs, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (5)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوٓا۟ إِن زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ لِلَّهِ مِن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٦)
Say, ‘O Jews! If you claim that you are Allah’s favourites, to the exclusion of other people, then long for death, should you be truthful.’ (6)
وَلَا يَتَمَنَّوْنَهُۥٓ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧)
Yet they will never long for it, because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (7)
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْمَوْتَ ٱلَّذِى تَفِرُّونَ مِنْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ مُلَـٰقِيكُمْ ۖ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
Say, ‘The death that you flee will indeed encounter you. Then you will be returned to the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, and He will inform you about what you used to do.’ (8)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to provide a profound exhortation toward prioritizing the matter of the Friday prayer (صلاة الجمعة) and fulfilling its obligatory requirements. It is among the magnified symbols of Allah (شعائر الله), and in its veneration and the attention given to it lies the rectification of people's Hereafter and their worldly life. The Exalted has proceeded to clarify His command by opening the discourse with His glorification and praise for the favor He bestowed upon an unlettered people (قوم أميين) by sending a Messenger from among them, who is himself unlettered, reciting His signs to them, purifying them through righteous deeds (صالحات الأعمال) and virtuous character (الزاكيات من الأخلاق), and teaching them the Book and Wisdom. Thus, he causes them to carry the Book of Allah and the gnoses of His religion (معارف دينه) in the best possible manner—both them and those believers who join them or succeed them thereafter. Let them, therefore, carry it in the best way, and let them beware of being like the Jews who were made to carry the Torah but then failed to carry its gnoses and rulings, becoming like a donkey carrying large tomes (أسفار).

Then, the discourse transitions to the command to leave off trade and hasten to the remembrance of Allah when the call to prayer is made on the day of Friday. He rebukes them for leaving the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) standing while delivering the sermon, dispersing and slipping away toward trade and diversion. This is a sign of their failure to carry the gnoses and rulings of the Book of Allah that they were charged with. The Surah is Medinan.

His saying, the Exalted: يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ الْمَلِكِ الْقُدُّوسِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth glorifies Allah, the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-mighty, the All-wise. Glorification (التسبيح) is the act of hallowing a thing and attributing to it purity and transcendence from defects and deficiencies. The use of the imperfect verb (المضارع) indicates continuity. The Sovereign (الملك) is the one who possesses the exclusive right to govern within the social order. The All-holy (القدوس) is an intensive form of holiness (القدس), which is transcendence and purity. The All-mighty (العزيز) is He whom no overcomer can defeat. The All-wise (الحكيم) is He whose actions are precise (المتقن), such that He does not act out of ignorance or haphazardly (جزاف).

In the verse, there is a demonstrative preamble and preparation for what is contained in His saying: "It is He who sent..." etc., regarding the sending of the Messenger to perfect humanity, bring them felicity, and guide them after they had been in manifest error.

This is because the Exalted is glorified and hallowed by the celestial and terrestrial existents by virtue of the deficiency they possess, for which He is the Completer, and the need they have, for which He is the Fulfiller. There is no deficiency or need except that He is the one hoped upon for its completion and fulfillment. Thus, He is the Glorified, transcendent above every deficiency and need. Therefore, He has the right to judge within the ontological order (نظام التكوين) among His creation as He wills, and within the legislative order (نظام التشريع) among His servants as He intends. How could it be otherwise? For He is a Sovereign (ملك) who has the right to rule over the people of His kingdom, and it is upon them to obey Him.

When He judges and legislates a religion among them, this does not arise from a need on His part to make them worship Him, nor from a deficiency in Him that is completed by their worship, for He is All-holy (قدوس), transcendent above every deficiency and need.

Furthermore, when He judges, legislates, and conveys it to them out of His self-sufficiency, calling them to it through His messengers, and they do not respond to His call but rebel against His obedience, this does not constitute an incapacitation of the Exalted by them. This is because He is the All-mighty (العزيز); no overcomer can defeat Him in what He intends.

Moreover, that which He has judged and legislated of the religion—inasmuch as He is the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-mighty—does not lead to futility (غي) without purpose. This is because He is the All-wise (الحكيم) in an absolute sense; He does not do what He does except for a benefit (مصلحة), and He does not desire from them what He desires except for a benefit that returns to them and a good they attain, whereby their state is rectified in their worldly life and their Hereafter.

In short, His legislating the religion and sending down the Book by raising a Messenger who conveys it to them by reciting His signs, purifying them, and teaching them, is a grace from the Exalted and a favor, as He said: "It is He who sent..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولاً مِنْهُمْ It is He who sent to the unlettered a messenger from among themselves... The unlettered (الأميون) is the plural of unlettered (أمي), which refers to one who does not read or write. What is meant by them—as has been said—are the Arabs, due to the scarcity of those among them who could read and write. The Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was from among them, meaning of their kind, which is distinct from his being sent to them; for he was from among them while being sent to all of humanity.

It has been suggested that the "unlettered" refers to those other than the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), as the Jews said—as Allah narrated from them: "There is no blame upon us in the matter of the unlettered (الأميين)" (3:75).

However, this is problematic as it does not suit His saying at the end of the verse: "reciting His signs to them..." etc., for he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not restrict any part of the call to those other than the Arabs and the People of the Book that he did not also present to them.

It has also been suggested that the "unlettered" refers to the people of Mecca, because they called it the Mother of Cities (أم القرى).

This is also problematic as it does not suit the Surah being Medinan, for it would imply that the pronouns in "purifies them and teaches them" refer to the Emigrants (المهاجرون) and those of the people of Mecca who embraced Islam after the Conquest and their successors, which is far from the style of the Quran.

There is no contradiction between his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being from the unlettered and raised among them, and his being sent to them and to others; this is obvious. His reciting His signs to them, his purifying them, and his teaching them the Book and Wisdom was because it was revealed in their language, and this was the first stage of his call. Therefore, when the call became somewhat established, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) began calling the Jews, Christians, and Magians, and wrote to the great leaders and kings.

Similarly, the call of Abraham and Ishmael (عليه‌السلام), as Allah the Exalted narrated: "Our Lord, make us both submissive to You, and of our progeny a submissive nation to You... Our Lord, raise in their midst a messenger from among them who shall recite to them Your signs and teach them the Book and Wisdom and purify them" (2:128-129), encompasses all the progeny of Ishmael from the Arabs of Mudar, whether they were from the people of Mecca or elsewhere. This does not contradict his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being sent to them and to others.

His saying: "reciting His signs to them" means the signs of His Book despite his being unlettered. This is an attribute of the Messenger.

His saying: "and purifies them and teaches them the Book and Wisdom." Purification (التزكية) is the verbal noun of zakah, meaning righteous growth (النمو الصالح) that is inseparable from goodness and blessing. Thus, his purifying them is his causing them to grow in a righteous manner by habituating them to virtuous character and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة). Through this, they become perfected in their humanity, their state is rectified in their worldly life and their Hereafter, they live felicitously, and they die felicitously.

Teaching the Book (تعليم الكتاب) is the clarification of the words of its verses and the interpretation of what is difficult therein. In contrast to it is the teaching of Wisdom (الحكمة), which refers to the true gnoses (المعارف الحقيقية) contained within the Quran. The Quran is referred to sometimes as "signs" (الآيات) and sometimes as "the Book" (الكتاب) to indicate that under each of these titles, it is a blessing for which gratitude is due—as has been said.

Purification has been mentioned here before the teaching of the Book and Wisdom, unlike what is found in the prayer of Abraham (عليه‌السلام), because this verse describes the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) upbringing (تربية) of the believers of his nation. In the station of upbringing, purification precedes the teaching of true sciences and authentic gnoses. As for what is in the prayer of Abraham (عليه‌السلام), it is a supplication and a request for this purification and knowledge of the Book and Wisdom to be realized in his progeny. In the stage of realization and characterization, sciences and gnoses are prior in rank and higher in degree than purification, which pertains to actions and character.

His saying: "although they were previously in manifest error." The word in (إِنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy inna (المخففة من الثقيلة). The meaning is that before the sending of the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), they were in manifest error. The verse is a praise following a glorification and is intended to express favor, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ and to others from among them who have not yet joined them. And He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. This is coordinated (عطف) with "the unlettered," and the pronoun in "from among them" refers back to them. The "from" (من) denotes partitivity (التبعيض). The meaning is: He raised [a messenger] among the unlettered and among others from them who have not yet joined them. And He is the All-mighty who is not overcome in His will, the All-wise who does not act futilely or haphazardly.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَاللهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is the Lord of great grace. The demonstrative "that" (ذلك) refers to the sending of the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and its significance has been magnified by the use of the distant demonstrative. Thus, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the one specifically chosen for this grace. The meaning is: That sending, and his reciting the signs of Allah, purifying the people, and teaching them the Book and Wisdom, is from the grace and gift of Allah, which He gives to whomever His will attaches. He has willed to give it to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and Allah is the Lord of great grace. This is what the commentators have said.

It is possible that "that" refers to the sending in terms of its relation to its various parties: the Sender, the Messenger, and those to whom he was sent. The meaning would be: That sending is from the grace of Allah, which He grants to whomever He wishes. He willed to specify Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with this grace, so He chose him as a Messenger; and He willed it for his nation, so He chose them for it, making him from among them and sending him to them.

This verse and the two verses before it—namely His saying: "It is He who sent..." until "...the Great"—are intended to express favor (الامتنان).

His saying, the Exalted: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْراةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوها كَمَثَلِ الْحِمارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفاراً The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then did not carry it, is that of the donkey carrying tomes... Al-Raghib said: Al-Safr (السفر)—with a fathah then a sukun—is the uncovering of a covering. This is specific to physical objects, such as uncovering the turban from the head or the veil from the face... until he said: Al-Sifr (السفر)—with a kasrah then a sukun—is the book that uncovers (يسفر) truths. The Exalted said: "is that of the donkey carrying tomes (أسفاراً)." End quote.

What is meant by being "charged with the Torah" (تحميل التوراة) is being taught it. What is meant by "carrying it" (حملها) is acting upon it, as supported by the context and evidenced by what is at the end of the verse: "Evil is the example of the people who deny the signs of Allah." Those who were charged with the Torah and then did not carry it are the Jews, to whose messenger Moses (عليه‌السلام) Allah revealed the Torah, teaching them the gnoses and laws therein. They then abandoned it and did not act upon it; thus, they carried it [physically/verbally] but did not carry it [practically]. So Allah struck for them the example of the donkey carrying tomes, which does not know the gnoses and truths within them; it gains nothing from carrying them except the exhaustion of bearing their weight.

The reason for the connection of this verse to what preceded it is that when the Exalted opened the discourse with the favor He bestowed upon the Muslims by sending an unlettered Prophet from among the unlettered, who recites to them the signs of His Book, purifies them, and teaches them the Book and Wisdom—thereby bringing them out from the darkness of error into the light of guidance, and from the abyss of ignorance to the pinnacle of knowledge and wisdom—He would later point out at the end of the Surah, by way of rebuke and reproach, what they did by dispersing and slipping away toward diversion and trade while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was standing delivering the sermon to them on the day of Friday. This constitutes a belittling of one of the greatest religious rites (المناسك الدينية) and reveals that they did not value it with its true worth, nor did they accord it its proper status.

Thus, Allah the Glorified interposed this parable and reminded them of the state of the Jews when they were charged with the Torah and then did not carry it, becoming like the donkey carrying tomes, not benefiting from the knowledge and wisdom therein. Therefore, they must prioritize the matter of religion, be mindful of Allah in their movements and stillness, venerate His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and honor him, and not belittle what he has brought. They should beware lest there befall them of His wrath what befell the Jews when they did not act upon what they knew, such that Allah counted them as ignorant wrongdoers (ظالمين) and likened them to a donkey carrying tomes.

In Ruh al-Ma'ani: The reason for the connection of the verse to what preceded it is its inclusion of an indication that this sent Messenger was sent by Allah the Exalted with the attributes described in the Torah and upon the tongues of the prophets of the Children of Israel. It is as if it were said: He is the one who sent the one whose glad tidings were in the Torah, described therein as the unlettered Prophet sent to an unlettered nation. The example of those to whom his description came in it and who knew it, yet did not believe in him, is like the example of the donkey. End quote.

You are well aware that this is an arbitrary claim (تحكم) for which there is no evidence from the perspective of the context (السياق).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ هادُوا إِنْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءُ لِلَّهِ مِنْ دُونِ النَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ Say, ‘O Jews! If you claim that you are Allah’s favorites, to the exclusion of other people, then long for death, if you are truthful.’ This is an argument against the Jews through which their falsehood is exposed in their claim that they are the favorites (أولياء) and beloved of Allah. Allah the Exalted has narrated what indicates this from them in His saying: "The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are Allah’s children and His beloved’" (5:18), and His saying: "Say, ‘If the abode of the Hereafter with Allah is exclusively for you, and not for other people, then long for death’" (2:94), and His saying: "And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise except one who is a Jew...’" (2:111).

The summary of the meaning is: Say to the Jews, addressing them: O you who have become Jews! If you believe that you are favorites of Allah to the exclusion of other people, if you are truthful in your claim, then long for death. For the favorite (الولي) loves to meet his patron, and whoever is certain that he is a favorite of Allah for whom Paradise is mandatory—with nothing between him and it except death—would love death and long for it to befall him, so that he may enter the Abode of Honor and be delivered from this lowly life, in which there is nothing but anxiety, grief, trial, and calamity.

It has been said: In His saying "favorites of Allah" (أَوْلِياءُ لِلَّهِ) without the possessive construction (إضافة), there is an indication that it is a mere claim of theirs without reality.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَتَمَنَّوْنَهُ أَبَداً بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالظَّالِمِينَ But they will never long for it because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. The Exalted informed His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that they would never long for it after He commanded him to offer them the longing for death.

He explained their lack of longing for death by "what their hands have sent ahead," which is a metaphor for wrongdoing (الظلم) and transgression (الفسوق). Thus, the meaning of the verse is: They will never long for death because of the wrongdoing their hands have sent ahead; thus they were wrongdoers (ظالمين), and Allah is All-knowing of the wrongdoers. He knows that they do not love to meet Him because they are His enemies; there is no friendship (ولاية) between Him and them, nor any love.

The two verses are similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: "Say, ‘If the abode of the Hereafter with Allah is exclusively for you, and not for other people, then long for death, if you are truthful.’ But they will never long for it because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers" (2:94-95).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ الْمَوْتَ الَّذِي تَفِرُّونَ مِنْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مُلاقِيكُمْ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلى عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ Say, ‘The death from which you flee will indeed encounter you. Then you will be returned to the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible, and He will inform you of what you used to do.’ The fa (ف) in His saying "it will indeed encounter you" (فَإِنَّهُ مُلاقِيكُمْ) carries the meaning of the response to a condition (جواب الشرط). It contains a threat to them that the death they detest—out of fear of being held accountable for the evil consequences of their deeds—will inevitably encounter them. Then they will be returned to their Lord, from whose garb of servitude they exited through their wrongdoings and whom they opposed with their actions. He is All-knowing of the reality of their deeds, both manifest and hidden, for He is the Knower of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception — and the Sensible (الشهادة) — that which is manifest to the senses. Thus, He will inform them of the reality of their deeds and their evil consequences, which are types of punishment.

In the verse, there is a notification to them: first, that their flight from death is an error on their part, for it will surely overtake and encounter them; second, that their detestation of meeting Allah is another error, for they are being returned to Him to be held accountable for their evil deeds; and third, that nothing of their deeds, manifest or hidden, is hidden from the Exalted, nor will their plotting avail them, for He is the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible.

The verse indicates: first, that death is a decreed truth, as He said: "Every soul shall taste death" (21:35), and He said: "We have ordained death among you, and We are not to be outstripped" (56:60).

Second, that the return to Allah for the accounting of deeds is a truth in which there is no doubt.

Third, that they will be made to stand before the reality of their deeds and will be fully recompensed for them.

Fourth, that nothing of their deeds is hidden from the Exalted. To indicate this, the name of Majesty (Allah) was substituted with His saying: "the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "It is He who sent to the unlettered a messenger from among themselves," narrated from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Mu'awiyah ibn 'Ammar, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the verse, he said: كانوا يكتبون ولكن لم يكن معهم كتاب من عند الله ولا بعث إليهم رسول فنسبهم الله إلى الأميين — "They used to write, but they did not have a Book from Allah, nor had a messenger been sent to them, so Allah attributed them to the unlettered (الأميين)."

And therein, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and to others from among them who have not yet joined them," he said: دخلوا الإسلام بعدهم — "They entered Islam after them."

In al-Majma', it is narrated: That the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse—and it was said to him: "Who are these?" He placed his hand on the shoulder of Salman and said: لو كان الإيمان بالثريا لنالته رجال من هؤلاء — "If faith were at the Pleiades, men from among these would surely attain it."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several collections of hadith, including Sahih al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa'i, from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and in it: He placed his hand on the head of Salman al-Farisi and said: والذي نفسي بيده لو كان العلم بالثريا ـ لناله رجال من هؤلاء — "By the One in whose hand is my soul, if knowledge were at the Pleiades, men from among these would surely attain it."

It was also narrated from Sa'id ibn Mansur and Ibn Mardawayh from Qays ibn Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: لو أن الإيمان بالثريا ـ لناله رجال من أهل فارس — "If faith were at the Pleiades, men from among the people of Persia would surely attain it."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: "The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then did not carry it, is that of the donkey..." he said: الحمار يحمل الكتب ولا يعلم ما فيها ـ ولا يعمل به ـ كذلك بنو إسرائيل قد حملوا مثل الحمار ـ لا يعلمون ما فيه ولا يعملون — "The donkey carries books but does not know what is in them, nor does it act upon them. Likewise, the Children of Israel were charged like the donkey—they do not know what is in it, nor do they act [upon it]."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah and al-Tabarani narrated from Ibn 'Abbas that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: من تكلم يوم الجمعة والإمام يخطب ـ فهو كالحمار يحمل أسفارا ـ والذي يقول له : أنصت ليس له جمعة — "Whoever speaks on Friday while the Imam is delivering the sermon is like the donkey carrying tomes, and the one who says to him 'Be silent' has no Friday [prayer reward]."

I say: In this, there is support for what we previously mentioned regarding the reason for the connection of the verse to what preceded it.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Say, ‘O Jews!’" the verse, he said: إن في التوراة مكتوب : أولياء الله يتمنون الموت — "Indeed, it is written in the Torah: The favorites of Allah long for death."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from 'Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: A man came to Abu Dharr and said: "O Abu Dharr, why is it that we detest death?" He replied: لأنكم عمرتم الدنيا وخربتم الآخرة ـ فتكرهون أن تنقلوا من عمران إلى خراب — "Because you have built up the world and destroyed the Hereafter; thus, you detest being moved from that which is built up to that which is in ruins."

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Teaching Wisdom)

Man, in his limited life that he spends in this world (النشأة), has no choice but to follow a way (سنة) that he adopts regarding what he desires and dislikes, and which he follows in his movements and stillness—in short, in all his strivings in life.

This way, in its type, follows the view man holds regarding the reality of the general cosmos and the reality of himself, and the connection between the two. This is evidenced by the variation we find in the ways and paths of nations according to the variation in their views regarding the reality of the origin of existence and man, who is a part of it.

Thus, he who does not see any existence beyond matter, restricts existence to the material, attributes existence to chance, and views man as a material composite with a life limited between birth and death, sees no felicity for himself except material felicity. He has no goal in his actions except material advantages such as wealth, children, status, and the like. He has no desire except to enjoy the goods of the world and to attain its material pleasures or what pertains to them, all of which end with death, which he considers the dissolution of the composition and annihilation.

However, he who sees the world as coming into being from a cause above it, transcendent beyond matter, and that beyond this abode is another abode, and after this world is the Hereafter—we find him differing in his way and path from the aforementioned group. In his actions, he seeks the felicity of the Hereafter beyond the felicity of this world. The forms of their actions, their goals, and their views differ from the first group.

The ways of these people also differ among themselves, such as the difference between the ways of the idolaters among the Brahmins, Buddhists, and others, and the followers of religions (المليين) among the Magians, Jews, Christians, and Muslims. For each is a direction toward which he turns.

In short, the follower of a religion (الملي) observes in his strivings the aspect of what he sees for himself of eternal, everlasting life. He adheres to views that suit this, such as his claim that man must prepare for the realm of permanence, turn toward his Lord, and not be excessive in occupying himself with the fleeting goods of the life of this world. The non-follower of religion, who is subservient to matter, turns toward the opposite of that. All of this is beyond doubt.

However, since man, by his material nature, is a captive of matter, oscillating between apparent causes, acting through them and being affected by them—one cause constantly pushing him to another without respite—he sees, according to what he imagines, that primacy (الأصالة) belongs to his interrupted worldly life. He believes that it and the goals and advantages it leads to are the final end and the ultimate purpose of his existence, for which he must strive to attain felicity.

Thus, the worldly life is the life, and what its people possess of property, blessing, desire, power, and honor is the reality in the true sense of the word. What they count as poverty, affliction, deprivation, weakness, humiliation, disaster, calamity, and loss is the reality. In short, everything the soul desires of immediate good or certain benefit is, in their view, absolute good and absolute benefit, and everything it does not desire is evil or harm.

Those among them who are not followers of a religion follow these views and have no news of what lies beyond. Those among them who are followers of a religion follow them in practice while professing the opposite in speech. Thus, they remain in a state of conflict between their speech and their action. The Exalted said: "Whenever it shines for them, they walk in it, and when it grows dark for them, they stand still" (2:20).

That which the Islamic call invites to in terms of belief and action is that which corresponds to the requirement of the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — upon which man was created and upon which his constitution is established, as He said: "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion" (30:30).

It is well known that the primordial nature is not guided in knowledge, nor does it incline in action, except toward that in which lies its actual perfection and true felicity. Thus, the fundamental beliefs it is guided to regarding the Origin and the Return, and the branch views and doctrines that stem from them, are true sciences and views that do not bypass the felicity of man; likewise, the actions it inclines toward.

Therefore, Allah the Exalted has named this religion based on the primordial nature as the "religion of truth" (دين الحق) in several places in His speech, such as His saying: "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the religion of truth" (61:9). He said regarding the Quran, which contains His call: "it guides to the truth" (46:30).

Truth (الحق) is nothing other than the view and belief that corresponds to reality and is accompanied by right-guidance (الرشد) without error (غي). This is Wisdom (الحكمة)—the view that is made firm (أحكم) in its truthfulness such that no falsehood interpenetrates it, and in its benefit such that no harm follows it. The Exalted pointed out that the call contains Wisdom in His saying: "Allah has sent down to you the Book and Wisdom" (4:113), and He described His revealed speech with it, saying: "By the wise Quran" (36:2), and He counted His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as a teacher of Wisdom in several places, such as His saying: "and teaches them the Book and Wisdom" (62:2).

Thus, the Quranic teaching undertaken by the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who clarifies what was sent down from Allah, is the teaching of Wisdom. Its function is to clarify what is the truth regarding the false, superstitious fundamental beliefs that have crept into people's understandings concerning the conception of the world of existence and the reality of man—who is a part of it—as previously indicated. It also clarifies what is the truth regarding the branch beliefs resulting from those fundamentals, which serve as the starting point for human actions and the titles for their goals and purposes.

People, for example, see that primacy belongs to their material life, such that their speaker said: "There is nothing but our life in this world" (45:24). The Quran alerts them with His saying: "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life" (24:64). They see that causes and means are what generate events and govern them—life, death, health, illness, wealth, poverty, blessing, affliction, provision, and deprivation—"rather, it was the plotting of night and day" (34:33). The Quran reminds them with His saying: "Look! To Him belong the creation and the command" (7:54), and His saying: "Sovereignty belongs only to Allah" (12:67), and other verses of Wisdom. They see that they have independence in will, doing what they wish, and the Quran corrects them with His saying: "But you do not wish unless Allah wishes" (76:30). They see that they have the choice to obey or disobey, to guide or be guided, and the Quran informs them with His saying: "You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes" (28:56).

They see that they have power, and the Quran denies this with His saying: "that power belongs entirely to Allah" (2:165).

They see that they have honor through wealth, children, and helpers, and the Quran judges otherwise with His saying: "Do they seek honor with them? Indeed, honor belongs entirely to Allah" (4:139), and His saying: "Yet all honor belongs to Allah and His Apostle, and the faithful" (63:8).

They see that being killed in the way of Allah is death and annihilation, and the Quran counts it as life when it says: "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware" (2:154). This is among other Quranic teachings with which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to call the people, as He said: "Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom" (16:125).

These are numerous sciences and views that the worldly life has depicted as their opposite in the souls of people and has beautified. The Exalted alerted to them in His Book, commanded His Messenger to teach them, and exhorted the believers to enjoin one another with them, as He said: "Indeed man is in loss, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth" (103:2-3), and He said: "He gives wisdom to whomever He wishes, and he who is given wisdom is certainly given an abundant good, and no one takes admonition except those who possess intellect" (2:269).

The Quran, in reality, casts man into a new mold in terms of knowledge and action, and shapes him anew, so that he lives a life that is never followed by death. To this, there is an indication in His saying, the Exalted: "Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (8:24), and His saying: "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose likeness is that of one in manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122).

We have clarified the aspect of Wisdom in each of its verses when addressing their interpretation, according to the scope of the discussion in the Book.

From what has preceded, the falsity of the claim of those who say that the interpretation (تفسير) of the Quran is merely its recitation, and that delving into the implications of the Quranic verses is part of the forbidden interpretation (التأويل), becomes clear. How far removed is such a claim!

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نُودِىَ لِلصَّلَوٰةِ مِن يَوْمِ ٱلْجُمُعَةِ فَٱسْعَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلْبَيْعَ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٩)
O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business. That is better for you, should you know. (9)
فَإِذَا قُضِيَتِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةُ فَٱنتَشِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٠)
And when the prayer is finished disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace, and remember Allah much so that you may be felicitous. (10)
وَإِذَا رَأَوْا۟ تِجَـٰرَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا ٱنفَضُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَآئِمًا ۚ قُلْ مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهْوِ وَمِنَ ٱلتِّجَـٰرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (١١)
When they sight a deal or a diversion, they scatter off towards it and leave you standing! Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and dealing, and Allah is the best of providers.’ (11)

(Commentary)

This section emphasizes the obligation of the Friday prayer (صلاة الجمعة) and the prohibition of trade (البيع) during its performance. It contains a rebuke for those who dispersed toward diversion and trade at that time, expressing disapproval of their conduct.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلاةِ مِنْ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ "O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business" (62:9) etc. What is meant by the "call for prayer on Friday" is the adhan (الأذان), as in His saying: وَإِذا نادَيْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلاةِ اتَّخَذُوها هُزُواً وَلَعِباً "When you make the call for prayer, they take it in derision and play" (5:58).

The word al-jumu‘ah (الجمعة) — with a dammah on both the jim and mim, or a dammah followed by a sukun — refers to one of the days of the week. It was originally called Yawm al-‘Arubah, but the name al-Jumu‘ah eventually became dominant. What is meant by "prayer on Friday" is the Friday prayer (صلاة الجمعة) prescribed for that day. "Hurrying" (السعي) refers to walking with speed. "The remembrance of Allah" (ذكر الله) refers to the prayer, as in His saying: وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ "and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater" (29:45), according to what has been said. Others have said it refers to the sermon (الخطبة) before the prayer. His saying: وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ "and leave all business" is a command to abandon it. Based on what the context suggests, it means a prohibition against engaging in any activity that distracts from the Friday prayer, whether it be trade or otherwise. The prohibition was specifically linked to trade (البيع) because it is among the most prominent instances of what distracts one from prayer.

The meaning is: O you who have faith, when the adhan is called for the Friday prayer on its day, be diligent in walking to the prayer and abandon trade and everything that distracts you from it.

His saying: ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "That is better for you, should you know" (62:9) is an exhortation and an inducement for them regarding what He commanded of prayer and the abandonment of trade.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَابْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ "Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace" (62:10) etc. What is meant by the "prayer being finished" is the performance of the Friday prayer. "Dispersing through the land" (الانتشار في الأرض) means scattering therein. "Seeking Allah’s grace" (ابتغاء فضل الله) refers to seeking sustenance (الرزق), considering its contrast with the abandonment of trade in the previous verse. However, it was previously mentioned that the intent is to abandon everything that distracts from the Friday prayer. Accordingly, "seeking Allah’s grace" refers to seeking His gift in a general sense through dispersing to seek His sustenance via buying and selling, as well as seeking His reward by visiting the sick, striving to fulfill the need of a fellow Muslim, visiting a brother in Allah, attending a gathering of knowledge, and the like.

His saying: فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ "disperse through the land" is a command occurring after a prohibition, thus it signifies permissibility (الجواز) and lawfulness (الإباحة) rather than obligation (الوجوب). The same applies to His saying: وَابْتَغُوا "and seek" and وَاذْكُرُوا "and remember."

His saying: وَاذْكُرُوا اللهَ كَثِيراً لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ "and remember Allah greatly so that you may come to felicity" (62:10). What is meant by "remembrance" (الذكر) is broader than verbal remembrance; it includes remembering Him, the Exalted, in the heart by turning toward Him inwardly. Felicity (الفلاح) — the attainment of salvation from all misery — in this context, considering the preceding discussion on purification (التزكية) and teaching (التعليم) and the severe rebuke in the following verse, refers to spiritual growth and knowledge. This is because frequent remembrance causes the meaning of the One remembered to become firmly rooted in the soul and inscribed in the mind, thereby severing the roots of heedlessness (الغفلة) and bequeathing religious godwariness (التقوى), which is the likely ground for felicity. The Exalted said: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ "and be wary of Allah so that you may come to felicity" (3:200).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً انْفَضُّوا إِلَيْها وَتَرَكُوكَ قائِماً "When they sight some merchandise or diversion, they scatter toward it and leave you standing" (62:11) etc. "Scattering" (الانفضاض) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is a metaphor for breaking apart and the separation of parts from one another.

The narrations of both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis agree that a caravan (عير) carrying merchandise arrived in Madinah on a Friday while the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was standing and delivering the sermon. They beat the drums and tambourines to inform the people, so the people in the mosque scattered toward them and left the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله standing and delivering the sermon; thus, the verse was revealed. So, "diversion" (اللهو) refers to the use of musical instruments and tools of entertainment to gather people for trade. The pronoun in "toward it" (إِلَيْها) refers to the merchandise (التجارة) because it was the primary objective, while the diversion was intended for its sake. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to one of the two, as if it were said: "they scattered toward it [the diversion] and they scattered toward it [the merchandise]." This is because each of them was a reason for the people to scatter toward it and gather around it. For this reason, He used the disjunctive "or" between them, saying: تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً "merchandise or diversion," and did not say "merchandise and diversion." The pronoun is suitable to refer to either because "diversion" (اللهو) is originally a verbal noun (مصدر) for which both masculine and feminine genders are permissible.

For this reason also, He considered "what is with Allah" (ما عِنْدَ اللهِ) better than each of them individually, saying: مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ "than diversion and than merchandise," and did not say "than diversion and merchandise."

His saying: قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ "Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and merchandise, and Allah is the best of providers’" (62:11) is a command to the Prophet to alert them to their error in what they did — and how heinous it was. What is meant by "what is with Allah" is the reward (الثواب) that follows listening to the sermon and the exhortation.

The meaning is: Say to them: What is with Allah of reward is better than diversion and merchandise, because His reward is a true, eternal, and uninterrupted good, whereas the good found in diversion and merchandise is an imaginary, vanishing, and false matter, and it may even entail His wrath, as is the case with diversion.

It has been said that "better" (خير) is used in the verse stripped of the meaning of comparison (التفضيل), as in His saying, the Exalted: أَأَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ "Are discrete lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" (12:39), and this is common in usage.

In the verse — meaning His saying: وَإِذا رَأَوْا "When they sight" — there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person (الغيبة). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to emphasize the rebuke and the disapproval of the act suggested by the context, by turning away from honoring them with direct address and leaving them in the state of absence (الغيبة), where their Lord does not face them with His Noble Countenance.

This turning away is also hinted at in His saying: قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ "Say, ‘What is with Allah is better’," where He did not specify to whom he should say it; He did not say "Say to them," just as He mentioned them with their pronoun at first without a preceding referent, saying: وَإِذا رَأَوْا "When they sight," relying on the indication of the context.

"The Best of Providers" (خير الرازقين) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah, like "The Provider" (الرزاق). The discussion on the meaning of "sustenance" (الرزق) has already preceded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Faqih, it is narrated: "In Madinah, when the mu’adhin called the adhan on Friday, a caller would announce: 'Trade is forbidden,' because of the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلاةِ مِنْ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ (O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business)."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn al-Mundhir, from Maymun ibn Mahran, and its wording is: "In Madinah, when the mu’adhin called the adhan on Friday, they would call out in the markets: 'Trade is forbidden! Trade is forbidden!'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ (hurry toward the remembrance of Allah), he said: "It means speed in walking." In a narration by Abu al-Jarud from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, it is said: "فاسعوا (hurry) means 'go forth'; and it is said: 'اسعوا (strive) means 'act for it,' which is trimming the mustache, plucking the armpits, clipping the nails, performing the ritual bath (الغسل), wearing the cleanest clothes, and applying perfume for Friday. This is the 'striving' (السعي) of which Allah says: وَمَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَسَعى لَها سَعْيَها وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ (Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an effort worthy of it, should he be faithful)."

I say: He means that "striving" (السعي) is not merely speed in walking.

In al-Majma‘, Anas narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said regarding His saying: فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ (Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land) — "It is not for seeking the world, but for visiting the sick, attending a funeral, and visiting a brother in Allah."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Jarir from Anas from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn ‘Abbas from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the same source, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "The prayer is on Friday, and the dispersing is on Saturday."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

In the same source, ‘Umar ibn Yazid narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) who said: "I indeed ride out for a need that Allah has already sufficed, and I only ride for it seeking that Allah may see me striving in the morning in search of the lawful (الحلال). Have you not heard the saying of Allah, exalted is His Name: فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَابْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ (Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace)?"

"Do you think that if a man entered a house and plastered its door shut, then said: 'My sustenance will descend upon me,' that this would happen? Indeed, he is one of the three whose supplication is not answered."

He said: I asked: "Who are these?" He replied: "A man who has a wife and prays against her; his prayer is not answered because her bond is in his hand — if he wished, he could set her free. And a man who has a right over another man but does not take witnesses against him, so the other denies him his right; he prays against him but is not answered because he abandoned what he was commanded to do. And a man who has something [wealth] but sits in his house and does not disperse, nor seek, nor search until he consumes it, then he supplicates and is not answered."

In the same source, Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah said: "A caravan arrived while we were praying with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so the people scattered toward it until only twelve men remained, of whom I was one. Then the verse was revealed: وَإِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً (When they sight some merchandise or diversion)."

From ‘Awali al-La’ali, Muqatil ibn Sulayman narrated: "While the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was delivering the sermon on Friday, Dihyah al-Kalbi arrived from Syria with merchandise. Whenever he arrived, no young woman (عاتق) remained in Madinah but that she came to him. He used to bring, whenever he arrived, everything the people needed — of flour, wheat, and other things. Then he would beat the drum to inform the people of his arrival, and the people would go out and buy from him."

"He arrived one Friday — and this was before he had embraced Islam — while the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was delivering the sermon on the pulpit. The people went out, and none remained in the mosque except twelve. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Were it not for these, stones would have been marked against them from the sky.' And Allah revealed the verse in Surah al-Jumu‘ah."

I say: The story is narrated through many chains from both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis, and the reports differ regarding the number of those who remained in the mosque, ranging from seven to forty.

In the same source: "انْفَضُّوا (they scattered)" means they dispersed. It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "They departed toward it and left you standing, delivering the sermon on the pulpit."

Jabir ibn Samurah said: "I never saw the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله deliver a sermon except while he was standing. So, whoever tells you that he delivered a sermon while sitting, deem him a liar."

I say: This is also narrated in other reports.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah recorded from Tawus who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله delivered the sermon standing, as did Abu Bakr, ‘Umar, and ‘Uthman. The first one to sit on the pulpit was Mu‘awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan."
